Meredith Castile’s Driver’s License is a short book exploring the driver’s license as a cultural object. I chose it to work with the major nonfiction assessment (Appendix 1). Students relate to the content of the book, and Castile models basic techniques of nonfiction, including transitions, summary, cataloguing, interviewing, quoting, and incorporating personal anecdotes. I like this book-assignment combination because it lets students choose an object that was important to them; lent itself to in-class exercises that students built into their essays; allowed students who feel uncomfortable sharing personal stories to focus on the object while giving those who liked disclosure room to get personal; and provided students with an approachable and clear focus: a simple object! Allowing students to choose their own object of interest breaks down a clear hierarchy and barriers around what traditional academic discourse can be.

For the fiction section of the course, I plan to use individual PDFs in a reading packet. After having the students read one sustained work, I want to expose them to a variety of short stories. This is inclusive of all students, even those for who the cost of many books would be a hardship.

I like the idea of my being able to call on quiet students who’ve made insightful observations to expand on their point, but I need to be mindful that this doesn’t make socially anxious or neurodivergent students uncomfortable. I also plan to use lit logs instead of these reading responses, because these will allow students to respond with a variety of media.
